title: podman run --rm will not remove the container if the command isn't found
body:
/kind bug

**Description**

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Run `podman run --rm` with some command that is not inside the container's path, e.g. `podman run --rm -it alpine xyz`. This occurs in both standard/root and rootless modes.

2. Observe that is says `internal libpod error`.

3. Run `podman ps -a` to see the list of present containers.

**Describe the results you received:**

The list of containers contains the one that I just ran.

**Describe the results you expected:**

It should not be on that list because `--rm` was used.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

As mentioned above, this occurs with or without root.

Example set of commands to reproduce (removed my shell ps1 because it looks ugly here):

```
$ sudo podman ps -a          
CONTAINER ID  IMAGE  COMMAND  CREATED  STATUS  PORTS  NAMES
$ sudo podman run --rm -it alpine xyz
container create failed: container_linux.go:344: starting container process caused "exec: \"xyz\": executable file not found in $PATH"
: internal libpod error
$ sudo podman ps -a                  
CONTAINER ID  IMAGE                            COMMAND  CREATED       STATUS   PORTS  NAMES
ddfe5395daa7  docker.io/library/alpine:latest  xyz      1 second ago  Created         vigilant_haibt
```

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:       0.12.1.1
Go Version:    go1.11.2
Git Commit:    "0c9259a2c84f6234a29dcd78674c3f4837fd3101"
Built:         Fri Dec  7 13:29:27 2018
OS/Arch:       linux/amd64
```

**Output of `podman info`:**

```
host:
  BuildahVersion: 1.6-dev
  Conmon:
    package: podman-0.12.1.1-1.git66d3499.fc29.x86_64
    path: /usr/libexec/podman/conmon
    version: 'conmon version 1.12.0-dev, commit: 0c9259a2c84f6234a29dcd78674c3f4837fd3101'
  Distribution:
    distribution: fedora
    version: "29"
  MemFree: 555073536
  MemTotal: 8222674944
  OCIRuntime:
    package: runc-1.0.0-59.dev.gitccb5efd.fc29.x86_64
    path: /usr/bin/runc
    version: |-
      runc version 1.0.0-rc6
      commit: 6e5a791a02fefb403034e0de8693d225d52b33a7
      spec: 1.0.1-dev
  SwapFree: 7964717056
  SwapTotal: 8359243776
  arch: amd64
  cpus: 4
  hostname: fedora.refi64.com
  kernel: 4.19.7-300.fc29.x86_64
  os: linux
  rootless: true
  uptime: 2h 6m 15.03s (Approximately 0.08 days)
insecure registries:
  registries: []
registries:
  registries:
  - docker.io
  - registry.fedoraproject.org

store:
  ContainerStore:
    number: 50
  GraphDriverName: vfs
  GraphOptions:
  - overlay.mount_program=/usr/bin/fuse-overlayfs
  GraphRoot: /var/home/ryan/.local/share/containers/storage
  GraphStatus: {}
  ImageStore:
    number: 137
  RunRoot: /run/user/1000/run
```

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

Reproduced on Fedora 29 Silverblue.

comments.body:
This is actually deliberate and mirrors Docker's behavior in the same situation. We retain the container for debugging purposes - you might want to go back and see what caused it to fail.

I'm not 100% convinced by that logic, but we'll probably leave it as-is to maintain compatibility.

Huh, I didn't realize that... Is there a particular reason you'd want to debug this, though? Like, maybe I could understand for something more complicated, but most of the time when the executable isn't found and you used `--rm`, it's just easier to use `podman run --rm` again than to try and look up the container ID to exec in it, and even then it's probably just typos that are immediately obvious (e.g. in my case I had run "mim" instead of "nim").

@mheon did docker's behavior change? It is contrary to what you stated.

```
[cloud-user@baude ~]$ sudo docker run -it --rm alpine foo
/usr/bin/docker-current: Error response from daemon: oci runtime error: container_linux.go:247: starting container process caused "exec: \"foo\": executable file not found in $PATH".
[cloud-user@baude ~]$ sudo docker ps -a
CONTAINER ID        IMAGE               COMMAND             CREATED             STATUS              PORTS               NAMES
[cloud-user@baude ~]$
```

@baude Huh. I know this issue came up before, and Docker compat was the reason we didn't end up changing it.

I'm not exactly committed to this functionality, so if it's not deliberately matching Docker behavior, I vote we scrap it and delete the container.
Yes I agree, I think we should scrap the container.  If a user wanted to examine it, he could just run the same command again with out the --rm.
